title: Altstadt-Lehel

infobox.value: Inhabitants | of which Foreigners | Area (ha.) | Inhabitants/ha. | Source

(German pronunciation: [[ˈaltˌʃtat]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Altstadt.ogg "File:De-Altstadt.ogg"), lit. 'old town'; [Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): *Oidstod*) and **Lehel** (German pronunciation: [[ˈlɛxl̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q188_(deu)-Ionenlaser-Lehel.wav "File:LL-Q188 (deu)-Ionenlaser-Lehel.wav"); [Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): *Lechl*) are districts of the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") city of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). Together they form the first borough of the city: **Altstadt-Lehel**.

in the north west of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as [Kaufingerstraße](/wiki/Kaufingerstra%C3%9Fe "Kaufingerstraße")/[Neuhauser Straße](/wiki/Neuhauser_Stra%C3%9Fe "Neuhauser Straße") in the south and Weinstraße/[Theatinerstraße](/wiki/Theatinerstra%C3%9Fe "Theatinerstraße") in the east. Located here was the centre of the clergy as there was a particularly high number of monasteries. The Kreuzviertel which was first mentioned via documents on 29 December 1458 is named after the Kreuzgasse: a street that today roughly corresponds to the Promenadenplatz and the Pacellistraße. The origin of the name is unclear. Other name: Eremitenviertel.

in the north east of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as Tal in the south and Dienerstraße in the west. The gentry preferred to reside here, probably because of the proximity to the Alten Hof. This town quarter was first mentioned on 29 December 1458. The name of the Graggenauer Quarter derives from Graggenau, which in turn has its root in the word "Krack", meaning Raven or Crow. Alternative name is Wilbrechts-Viertel, after the first *Viertelhauptmann* (translates as "quarter captain"). Until into the 16th century the local tax records considered the area outside the wall up to the modern-day Prinzregentenstrasse as part of Graggenau.

in the south east of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as Tal in the north and [Sendlinger Straße](/wiki/Sendlinger_Stra%C3%9Fe "Sendlinger Straße") in the west. Traders predominantly resided here. First mention via documentation of the area is veritable as of 15 September 1508. The name Anger (meaning Meadow, however with a limitation to common land) derives from the meadow which was in domain of the modern-day St. Jakobs Platz with the Münchner Stadtmuseum in the old armoury and the new [Jewish centre](/wiki/Ohel_Jakob_synagogue "Ohel Jakob synagogue") (*Jüdisches Zentrum München*). Another name for the district is Rindermarktviertel, after the former cattle market of the city at the site of the Altstadt on which the [Rindermarktbrunnen](/wiki/Rindermarktbrunnen "Rindermarktbrunnen"), sculptured in 1964 by Josef Hensel, is a reminder of the former significance of the area.

in the south west of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as Kaufingerstraße/Neuhauserstraße in the north and Sendlinger Straße in the south. Traders also predominantly resided here. The name was first documented on 29 December 1458. On Altheimer Eck (Corner/Nook of Altheim) in the Hackenviertel was the area of Altheim, which was included into the area via the defensive wall in around 1285. The name is derived from Hacken, which apart from meaning hoeing or chopping, is also like a closed, fenced-in area. (Compare "Hecke", meaning hedge). Alternative name for Hackenviertel is Kramerviertel.
as it is written, with a lightly accented e on the first syllable in the first aspirate, and shortly pronounced e in the second syllable.

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [48°08′10″N 11°34′20″E﻿ / ﻿48.13611°N 11.57222°E﻿ / 48.13611; 11.57222](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Altstadt-Lehel&params=48_08_10_N_11_34_20_E_region:DE-BY_type:city_source:dewiki)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Borough of Munich, Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_01_%28Karte%29_-_Altstadt_-_Lehel.png/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_01_%28Karte%29_-_Altstadt_-_Lehel.png)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_01_(Karte)_-_Altstadt_-_Lehel.png)

Position of Altstadt-Lehel in Munich

**Altstadt** (German pronunciation: [[ˈaltˌʃtat]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Altstadt.ogg "File:De-Altstadt.ogg"), lit. 'old town'; [Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): *Oidstod*) and **Lehel** (German pronunciation: [[ˈlɛxl̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q188_(deu)-Ionenlaser-Lehel.wav "File:LL-Q188 (deu)-Ionenlaser-Lehel.wav"); [Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): *Lechl*) are districts of the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") city of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). Together they form the first borough of the city: **Altstadt-Lehel**.

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Location")]

The borough covers the historical area of [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Munich) "Altstadt (Munich)") (as defined by the [Altstadtring](/wiki/Altstadtring "Altstadtring")) and the Lehel area, which is attached to Altstadt via the north east. It also covers the [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") in the east and the [Englischer Garten](/wiki/Englischer_Garten "Englischer Garten") as well as [Prinzregentenstraße](/wiki/Prinzregentenstra%C3%9Fe_(Munich) "Prinzregentenstraße (Munich)"), bordering it in the north.

## History and description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History and description")]

### Altstadt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Altstadt")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Frauenkirche_and_Neues_Rathaus_Munich_March_2013.JPG/250px-Frauenkirche_and_Neues_Rathaus_Munich_March_2013.JPG)](/wiki/File:Frauenkirche_and_Neues_Rathaus_Munich_March_2013.JPG)

Altstadt

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Luftbild_M%C3%BCnchen_Innenstadt.jpg/250px-Luftbild_M%C3%BCnchen_Innenstadt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luftbild_M%C3%BCnchen_Innenstadt.jpg)

Aerial image of Altstadt-Lehel

Since the town extension via [Ludwig the Bavarian](/wiki/Louis_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Louis IV, Holy Roman Emperor") lasting from 1285 until 1347, Altstadt consisted of four quarters and an open locale:

* The **Kreuzviertel** in the north west of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as [Kaufingerstraße](/wiki/Kaufingerstra%C3%9Fe "Kaufingerstraße")/[Neuhauser Straße](/wiki/Neuhauser_Stra%C3%9Fe "Neuhauser Straße") in the south and Weinstraße/[Theatinerstraße](/wiki/Theatinerstra%C3%9Fe "Theatinerstraße") in the east. Located here was the centre of the clergy as there was a particularly high number of monasteries. The Kreuzviertel which was first mentioned via documents on 29 December 1458 is named after the Kreuzgasse: a street that today roughly corresponds to the Promenadenplatz and the Pacellistraße. The origin of the name is unclear. Other name: Eremitenviertel.
* The **Graggenauer Viertel** in the north east of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as Tal in the south and Dienerstraße in the west. The gentry preferred to reside here, probably because of the proximity to the Alten Hof. This town quarter was first mentioned on 29 December 1458. The name of the Graggenauer Quarter derives from Graggenau, which in turn has its root in the word "Krack", meaning Raven or Crow. Alternative name is Wilbrechts-Viertel, after the first *Viertelhauptmann* (translates as "quarter captain"). Until into the 16th century the local tax records considered the area outside the wall up to the modern-day Prinzregentenstrasse as part of Graggenau.
* The **Angerviertel** in the south east of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as Tal in the north and [Sendlinger Straße](/wiki/Sendlinger_Stra%C3%9Fe "Sendlinger Straße") in the west. Traders predominantly resided here. First mention via documentation of the area is veritable as of 15 September 1508. The name Anger (meaning Meadow, however with a limitation to common land) derives from the meadow which was in domain of the modern-day St. Jakobs Platz with the Münchner Stadtmuseum in the old armoury and the new [Jewish centre](/wiki/Ohel_Jakob_synagogue "Ohel Jakob synagogue") (*Jüdisches Zentrum München*). Another name for the district is Rindermarktviertel, after the former cattle market of the city at the site of the Altstadt on which the [Rindermarktbrunnen](/wiki/Rindermarktbrunnen "Rindermarktbrunnen"), sculptured in 1964 by Josef Hensel, is a reminder of the former significance of the area.
* The **Hackenviertel** in the south west of Altstadt. Its borders are described roughly as Kaufingerstraße/Neuhauserstraße in the north and Sendlinger Straße in the south. Traders also predominantly resided here. The name was first documented on 29 December 1458. On Altheimer Eck (Corner/Nook of Altheim) in the Hackenviertel was the area of Altheim, which was included into the area via the defensive wall in around 1285. The name is derived from Hacken, which apart from meaning hoeing or chopping, is also like a closed, fenced-in area. (Compare "Hecke", meaning hedge). Alternative name for Hackenviertel is Kramerviertel.

### Lehel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Lehel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Marienplatz_%288273835825%29.jpg/250px-Marienplatz_%288273835825%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marienplatz_(8273835825).jpg)

View from Altstadt towards Lehel

#### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/M%C3%BCnchen_-_Isar_%2824542516635%29.jpg/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_-_Isar_%2824542516635%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_-_Isar_(24542516635).jpg)

One arm of the Isar river in Lehel

"(The) Lehel" (with locals, the article will never be missing) is regarded "the oldest suburb of Munich"; it was, however, officially incorporated into the city as of 1724 only.

Lehel has become another area for the arts next to the [Kunstareal](/wiki/Kunstareal "Kunstareal"): The *[State Museum of Ethnology](/wiki/State_Museum_of_Ethnology "State Museum of Ethnology")* in [Maximilianstraße](/wiki/Maximilianstrasse_(Munich) "Maximilianstrasse (Munich)") is the second largest collection in Germany of artifacts and objects from outside Europe, while the *[Bavarian National Museum](/wiki/Bavarian_National_Museum "Bavarian National Museum")* and the adjoining *State Archeological Collections* in Prinzregentenstrasse rank among Europe's major art and cultural history museums. The nearby *[Schackgalerie](/wiki/Schackgalerie "Schackgalerie")* is an important gallery of German 19th-century paintings.

The quarter, having been one of Munich's surrounding poor houses in times of old, began to turn into today's much sought-after and very expensive residential area beginning with the construction of the first Wilhelminian style apartment houses at the turn of the 20th century and latest after the start of heavy gentrification during the 1980s.

#### Origin of the name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Origin of the name")]

There are many theories for the origin of the name of this area.

The more commonly represented indicates to a connection with the name "Lohe", [Central](/wiki/Central_German "Central German") and [Upper German](/wiki/Upper_German "Upper German") for a more or less dwindling alluvial forest and/or a light grove intermingled with shrubbing/coppice. Growing predominantly on a ground made up of gravel with heath upon it, it used to be found all around historic Munich from the west to the river Isar in the north-east on the Munich gravel plain. It meets with many locations and its remainders can still be found in the spot from Aubing to Eching. Many places' names in the area still refer to these forests, for example Auginger Lohe. Angerlohe, Allacher Lohe, Lohhof or Keferloh. Here is the consideration, that the pharyngeally pronounced word Lohe, thus perhaps Loche, adjusted and led Orthography in the course of time for other own place names, for example Lochham or Lochhausen. Hereafter, the [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") diminutive *Löhel*, modernly, if even somewhat crudely spelled "Lehel", refers to a small bit of said alluvial forest. In fact, Lehel used to be an "island" in a geographical sense, as it was surrounded by the river [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") at one side and brooks running alongside and towards the river at the others.

This is congruent with the fact that old inhabitants of the district used to pronounce their district as Lächl, with a long vocal. Only in recent times, firstly in a failed attempt to adapt an [Old Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") dialect word to [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") by order of town officials and decision of the local public transport compound and secondly by the many non-native inhabitants having adapted to this and native youngsters no longer commanding the dialect, has the pronunciation shifted towards **Lehel** as it is written, with a lightly accented e on the first syllable in the first aspirate, and shortly pronounced e in the second syllable.

Another, rarer theory holds the view that Lehel is possibly in regards to an earlier feud ("Lehen) that took place; thus Lehen becoming Lehel through the [Bavarian](/wiki/Austro-Bavarian "Austro-Bavarian") [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive"). The author György Dalos mentions in his book "Hungary in a nutshell" the execution of the Hungarian army commander Lehel in the year 955 at [Regensburg](/wiki/Regensburg "Regensburg") and thus this district of Munich was named after this hapless warrior.

## Statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Statistics")]

(as of 31 December, inhabitants with principal residence)

| Year | Inhabitants | of which Foreigners | Area (ha.) | Inhabitants/ha. | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *2000* | 18,374 | 4,219 (23.0%) | 316.36 | 58 | [*Statistisches Taschenbuch München 2001.*](https://web.archive.org/web/20060221184436/http://www.muenchen.info/sta/m-stat/themen/stadtbezirke/stadtbezirkszahlen_2000/taschenbuch_2001.pdf#page=17&pagemode=none&view=fit) pdf-Download |
| *2001* | 18,462 | 4,227 (22.9%) | 316.36 | 58 | [*Statistisches Taschenbuch München 2002.*](https://web.archive.org/web/20061210205113/http://www.muenchen.info/sta/m-stat/themen/stadtbezirke/stadtbezirkszahlen_2001/taschenbuch_2002.pdf#page=17&pagemode=none&view=fit) pdf-Download |
| *2002* | 18,193 | 4,079 (22.4%) | 316.36 | 58 | [*Statistisches Taschenbuch München 2003.*](https://web.archive.org/web/20061210205412/http://www.muenchen.info/sta/m-stat/themen/stadtbezirke/stadtbezirkszahlen_2002/taschenbuch_2003.pdf#page=17&pagemode=none&view=fit) pdf-Download |
| *2003* | 18,159 | 4,178 (23.0%) | 316.35 | 57 | [*Statistisches Taschenbuch München 2004.*](https://web.archive.org/web/20061210205442/http://www.muenchen.info/sta/m-stat/themen/stadtbezirke/stadtbezirkszahlen_2003/taschenbuch_2004.pdf#page=17&pagemode=none&view=fit) pdf-Download |
| *2004* | 18,210 | 4,108 (22.6%) | 316.39 | 58 | [*Statistisches Taschenbuch München 2005.*](https://web.archive.org/web/20061111015824/http://www.muenchen.info/sta/m-stat/themen/stadtbezirke/stadtbezirkszahlen_2004/taschenbuch_2005.pdf#page=17&pagemode=none&view=fit) pdf-Download |
| *2005* | 18,631 | 4,261 (22.9%) | 315.87 | 59 | [*Statistisches Taschenbuch München 2006.*](https://web.archive.org/web/20070128064752/http://www.muenchen.info/sta/m-stat/themen/stadtbezirke/stadtbezirkszahlen_2005/taschenbuch_2006.pdf#page=17&pagemode=none&view=fit) pdf-Download |

In 2004, with an area of 316.39 [hectares](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") there yields 58 inhabitants per hectare.

(*Statistical Pocket Book of Munich*)

## Miscellaneous

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Miscellaneous")]

Houses of Altstadt in a backyard of Widenmayer Street in Lehel, later demolished prior to the construction of and substituted by an insurance trust's giant office building, served as the backdrop of the joiner's workshop in *Meister Eder und sein [Pumuckl](/wiki/Pumuckl "Pumuckl")*.

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altstadt-Lehel&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Literature")]

* Helmuth Stahleder: *Von Allach bis Zamilapark*. Names and historical basic information of the history of Munich and its incorporated suburbs. [Stadtarchiv München](/wiki/Stadtarchiv_M%C3%BCnchen "Stadtarchiv München"), ed. München: Buchendorfer Verlag 2001. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-934036-46-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-934036-46-5 "Special:BookSources/3-934036-46-5")
